name: Rawlsian Reformer
description: A principled egalitarian who insists on fair procedures and universal moral standards. Deeply pluralist and institutionalist in style, they reject zero-sum thinking and tribal politics. Approaches reform through rules-based statecraft, never compromising on the idea that justice requires both redistribution and procedural integrity.

name: Independent Social Democrat
description: A quiet redistributionist who favors broad social insurance without ideological fanfare. Universalist in moral outlook but moderate in intensity, they trust institutions and prefer pragmatic coalitions over movement politics. Not strongly tribal or partisan — just consistently left-of-center on economics.

name: Consensus Redistributionist
description: A technocratic progressive who pairs redistributionist economics with evidence-based policymaking. Strongly procedural, consensus-seeking, and deeply engaged, they trust empirical data over tradition or intuition. Rejects the fighter aesthetic — prefers competent administration to political combat.

name: Labor Reformer
description: A bread-and-butter labor advocate who channels redistributionist energy through organized political action. Highly engaged and procedurally oriented, they favor the fighter aesthetic over statesmanship. Sees politics through a class lens, with moderate partisan identity and real loyalty to working-class institutions.

name: Pluralist Structuralist
description: A rules-driven reformer committed to pluralism and broad moral inclusion. Sees the system as deeply flawed yet believes in positive-sum cooperation. Prefers institutional solutions and statesman-style leadership, rejecting tribal loyalties. A quiet but determined guardian of the public interest.

name: Fairness Pragmatist
description: A pragmatic empiricist who leads with data, not ideology. Strongly consensus-seeking and procedural, with moderate economic preferences and broad moral tolerance. Rejects traditionalist and intuitionist epistemology entirely. Favors quiet competence and statesmanship, staying engaged without becoming tribal or partisan.

name: Solidarist Reformer
description: A culturally traditional redistributionist who bridges left economics with conservative values. Consensus-seeking and morally universalist, they draw on traditionalist epistemology and statesman aesthetics. Rejects both the fighter style and empiricist coldness — solidarity means community bonds, not just policy numbers.

name: Procedural Redistributionist
description: A local-government redistributionist who trusts institutions and procedures to deliver fairness. Strongly engaged and procedural, with an institutionalist worldview and statesman aesthetic. Not highly tribal or partisan — focused on making municipal systems work equitably for ordinary people.

name: Bread-and-Butter Progressive
description: A kitchen-table progressive focused on wages, healthcare, and pocketbook issues. Strongly assimilationist on culture and indifferent to cultural debates. Rejects free-market economics with conviction. Moderate engagement level — shows up when material interests are at stake, not for abstract causes.

name: Jacobin Egalitarian
description: A fiery egalitarian who sees politics as zero-sum class struggle. Uncompromising, outcome-focused, and deeply culturally progressive. Relies on intuitionist epistemology and the fighter aesthetic, rejecting statesmanship and technocracy as complicit moderation. Highly engaged, with a revolutionary sense of urgency.

name: Class-War Leftist
description: A hard-left materialist who views society through irreconcilable class conflict. Uncompromising and deeply pessimistic about human nature, yet sees the system as rigged and in need of radical overhaul. Tribal, combative, and fully engaged — rejects consensus as capitulation to the ruling class.

name: Radical Leveler
description: A radical who prioritizes cultural progressivism and systemic critique over material redistribution alone. Outcome-focused and anti-procedural, they see the existing system as deeply broken. Uses the fighter aesthetic and autonomous epistemology. Highly engaged and structurally dissatisfied, pushing for sweeping transformation.

name: Activist Progressive
description: A movement-oriented egalitarian who combines redistributionist economics with visionary politics. Highly engaged and zero-sum in worldview, but more open to coalition than pure radicals. Blends autonomous epistemology with an idealistic aesthetic — sees change as coming through collective mobilization, not institutions.

name: Moral Firebrand
description: A moral crusader who combines extreme cultural progressivism with burning universalist conviction. Deeply pluralist, optimistic about humanity, and sees politics through an uncompromising visionary lens. Anti-procedural and non-partisan, they reject both the system and any compromise that dilutes moral principle.

name: Insurgent Equalizer
description: An insurgent redistributionist who fights structural injustice through radical action. Deeply pluralist and universalist but completely outcome-focused, with zero tolerance for compromise. Sees the system as broken, politics as zero-sum, and tribal solidarity as essential to the struggle.

name: Uncompromising Redistributionist
description: A hard-line redistributionist who refuses to budge on economics or cultural progressivism. Deeply pessimistic about human nature and uncompromising in approach. Sees politics as zero-sum class warfare but channels it through principled conviction rather than tribal identity. Highly engaged and system-critical.

name: Anarchist Mutualist
description: A stateless-society idealist who pairs redistributionist economics with radical anti-proceduralism and positive-sum thinking. Deeply pluralist, non-partisan, and optimistic about human cooperation. Gravitates toward the pastoral aesthetic — preferring organic community over any form of institutional authority.

name: Grassroots Autonomist
description: A flat-hierarchy progressive who rejects both partisan identity and tribal loyalty outright. Redistributionist and pluralist, but driven by authentic individual expression rather than organizational discipline. System-critical and outcome-focused, they prefer direct action to working within established political structures.

name: Principled Cosmopolitan
description: A moral universalist who applies principled reasoning across borders. Culturally progressive, pluralist, and deeply anti-tribal. Trusts institutions and sees politics as positive-sum, but remains moderate on economic extremes. Combines institutionalist epistemology with a visionary aesthetic — principled idealism grounded in careful reasoning.

name: Pluralist Universalist
description: A pluralist who defends cultural diversity and cooperative politics with genuine engagement. Redistributionist-leaning, culturally progressive, and strongly anti-tribal. Sees politics as positive-sum and trusts institutional frameworks. A quiet institutionalist who shows up when universal values are at stake.

name: Rights Cosmopolitan
description: A rights-absolutist cosmopolitan who defines politics through universal moral principles. Deeply culturally progressive, pluralist, and uncompromising — refuses to bend on rights even for consensus. Optimistic about humanity but low on partisan identity. A principled visionary who rejects tribal thinking entirely.

name: Ethical Internationalist
description: An internationalist who combines redistributionist economics with deep consensus-seeking and positive-sum worldview. Strongly progressive, pluralist, and optimistic about humanity. Highly engaged with an institutionalist-visionary style — believes global cooperation and ethical governance can solve humanity's deepest problems.

name: World-Minded Reformer
description: A global-minded redistributionist who champions pluralism and universalist morality with reformist energy. Strongly procedural and anti-tribal, with an institutionalist-technocratic style. Sees the system as somewhat broken but believes sustained engagement and evidence-based reform can build a more just world.

name: Cosmopolitan Pragmatist
description: A practically minded cosmopolitan who values cultural progressivism and pluralism without rigid ideology. Consensus-seeking and anti-tribal, with positive-sum instincts and moderate economic views. Works within systems rather than against them — a quiet pragmatist with internationalist sensibilities and no appetite for culture wars.

name: Popperian Liberal
description: A proceduralist liberal who trusts open inquiry and falsification over tradition or gut feeling. Strongly empiricist, anti-tribal, and optimistic about human nature. Rejects partisan identity and sees politics as positive-sum. Favors the statesman aesthetic — measured, principled reform through transparent institutions.

name: Global Caretaker
description: A globally oriented caretaker who combines redistributionist economics with culturally progressive pluralism and optimism. Positive-sum, anti-tribal, and consensus-inclined. Draws on institutionalist epistemology and pastoral warmth — approaches global problems with compassion and a faith in cooperative, evidence-informed solutions.

name: Liberationist Progressive
description: A liberationist who pairs redistributionist economics with radical moral universalism and outcome-first urgency. Deeply anti-tribal, culturally progressive, and positive-sum in worldview. Highly engaged with intuitionist epistemology and a visionary edge — demands liberation now, not gradual institutional reform.

name: Planetary Steward
description: A planetary thinker who pairs redistributionist economics with deep cultural progressivism and systemic critique. Pluralist, universalist, and anti-tribal, with empiricist epistemology and a pastoral sensibility. Sees both human nature and social systems as fixable — optimistic about big-picture ecological and social reform.

name: Hamiltonian Technocrat
description: A data-driven statist who believes expert-led institutions can optimize society. Culturally progressive but moderate on economics, deeply procedural and consensus-oriented. Strongly empiricist-technocratic, anti-tribal, and allergic to partisan identity. Treats politics as engineering — find what works, implement it efficiently.

name: Systems Modernizer
description: A systems-oriented progressive who combines deep cultural progressivism with procedural discipline and positive-sum confidence. Uncompromising on process, empiricist in method, and visionary in aspiration. Anti-tribal, anti-zero-sum — believes the right institutional architecture can unlock human cooperation at scale.

name: Administrative Liberal
description: An administrative-minded liberal who trusts procedures, institutions, and universalist principles. Mildly redistributionist and culturally centrist, with a technocratic style and empiricist epistemology. Anti-tribal and moderately engaged — believes well-designed government programs, not movements, deliver lasting progressive outcomes.

name: Institutional Optimizer
description: An institutional optimizer who leans market-friendly but leads with empiricist-technocratic method. Deeply consensus-seeking, procedural, and anti-tribal. Believes in positive-sum cooperation and working within systems. More concerned with getting processes right than with ideological positioning on economics or culture.

name: Fabian Modernizer
description: A patient gradualist who combines deep proceduralism with optimism about human progress. Institutionalist in epistemology and technocratic in style. Redistributionist but unhurried, anti-tribal and anti-partisan — trusts that steady, evidence-informed reform through established channels produces more durable change than any revolution.

name: Data-Driven Moderate
description: A data-first moderate who treats ideology as noise and evidence as signal. Assimilationist on culture, procedurally inclined, and system-trusting. Indifferent to partisan identity and material debates — lets the numbers guide policy. Empiricist-technocratic in style, with allergic reactions to tribal thinking.

name: Reform Engineer
description: A reform-minded engineer who pairs market-friendly economics with cultural progressivism and deep procedural commitment. Strongly empiricist-technocratic, anti-tribal, and highly engaged. Approaches governance as a design problem — optimize the system, test the results, scale what works.

name: Localist Progressive
description: A community-focused progressive who grounds left-leaning economics in local relationships and traditionalist-pastoral sensibility. Assimilationist on culture, with modest engagement and no tribal or partisan attachments. Prefers small-scale, grounded reform to grand national programs — politics should serve the neighborhood.

name: Quiet Egalitarian
description: A neighborly egalitarian who combines strong redistributionist conviction with traditionalist epistemology and pastoral warmth. Universalist in morality but rooted in community bonds, with low engagement and no partisan identity. Believes economic fairness starts at the local level, through care rather than conflict.

name: Rooted Social Reformer
description: A rooted reformer who pairs redistributionist economics with traditional cultural values and consensus-seeking pragmatism. Traditionalist-pastoral in style and low-key in engagement — no culture warrior, just a community-minded progressive who wants economic justice within established cultural institutions.

name: Pastoral Leftist
description: A pastoral leftist who blends redistributionist leanings with traditional cultural values and genuine optimism. Positive-sum, low-tribal, and universalist in morality. Traditionalist-pastoral in both epistemology and style — sees economic justice as flowing naturally from caring, rooted community life.

name: Solidaristic Localist
description: A community solidarity builder who channels modest economic progressivism through local networks and tribal loyalty. Traditionalist-pastoral in style, highly engaged, and cooperative. Sees politics as fundamentally local and collective — rooted in neighborhood bonds rather than abstract ideology or national movements.

name: Moral Egalitarian
description: A paternalistic egalitarian who combines strong redistributionist economics with traditional cultural values and universalist morality. Traditionalist-pastoral in style, moderate in engagement, and low on tribal or partisan identity. Believes the community has a duty to ensure material security for its members.

name: Traditionalist Moralist
description: A faith-motivated progressive who combines redistributionist leanings with traditional cultural conservatism and universalist moral commitments. Traditionalist-pastoral in both epistemology and style. Not politically engaged or tribal — draws on religious conviction, not party ideology, as the basis for economic justice.

name: Systemic Redistributionist
description: An ecology-first progressive who pairs redistributionist economics with system-critical thinking and rooted community values. Traditionalist-pastoral in style, moderate on culture, and engaged with questions of sustainability. Believes structural reform must serve both material equality and environmental stewardship.

name: Consensus Builder
description: A consummate bridge-builder whose defining trait is extreme consensus-seeking. Centrist on economics and culture, deeply procedural, positive-sum, and anti-tribal. Institutionalist in epistemology and statesman in style — believes patient negotiation and institutional processes can resolve nearly any political disagreement.

name: Arbiter Moderate
description: A judicial-temperament moderate whose highest priorities are proceduralism, moral universalism, and human optimism. Deeply technocratic and anti-tribal, with moderate positions across economics and culture. Approaches politics as an arbiter — weighing evidence, seeking fairness, and trusting institutions to deliver just outcomes.

name: Institutional Centrist
description: An institutional centrist who combines traditional cultural leanings with deep pluralism and fierce anti-zero-sum conviction. System-trusting, anti-tribal, and engaged. Institutionalist in epistemology and statesman in style — believes existing institutions, properly maintained, can accommodate diversity without sacrificing stability.

name: Temperate Pluralist
description: A temperate pluralist who favors cultural openness, progressive values, and procedural fairness without strong partisan or tribal attachments. Anti-tribal, positive-sum, and consensus-oriented. Institutionalist in epistemology and statesman in style — a quiet moderate who values coexistence and measured engagement.

name: Public-Minded Moderate
description: A public-minded moderate who prioritizes universalist morality and procedural fairness above partisan loyalty. Economically centrist, culturally moderate, and anti-tribal. Institutionalist in epistemology and statesman in style — engages politically out of civic duty, not ideological passion or group identity.

name: Hinge Citizen
description: A true swing voter whose political identity is defined by moderate centrism across every dimension. Deeply pluralist in outlook, institutionalist in epistemology, and statesman in style. Anti-tribal and anti-extreme — gravitates toward whoever seems most reasonable, most procedural, and most willing to compromise.

name: Millian Liberal
description: A classical liberal who pairs free-market economics with cultural progressivism, deep pluralism, and radical optimism about human nature. Autonomous in epistemology and technocratic in style — trusts individual reason over institutions or tradition. Anti-tribal, anti-zero-sum, and moderate in partisan attachment.

name: Meritocratic Liberal
description: A meritocratic liberal who trusts free markets and procedural fairness to reward talent. Deeply procedural, empiricist-technocratic, and anti-tribal. Low-intensity across most dimensions — cares mainly that the rules are fair, the data is respected, and outcomes follow from individual effort and evidence-based policy.

name: Enterprise Pluralist
description: A business-friendly pluralist who pairs free-market economics with consensus-seeking and positive-sum optimism. Anti-tribal and empiricist, with a visionary streak. Believes competitive markets, open societies, and pragmatic cooperation create the conditions for broad prosperity — not through redistribution, but through enterprise.

name: Opportunity Liberal
description: An opportunity-focused liberal who rejects both rigid procedures and zero-sum thinking. Deeply optimistic about human nature, pluralist, and anti-tribal. Empiricist in epistemology with a strong visionary aesthetic — believes removing barriers and empowering individuals creates more prosperity than any redistributionist program.

name: Free-Exchange Modernist
description: A free-market cosmopolitan who treats economic liberty and cultural pluralism as inseparable. Strongly anti-procedural, anti-tribal, and positive-sum, with deep empiricist epistemology and visionary style. Sees free exchange — of goods, ideas, and cultures — as the engine of progress. Allergic to borders and bureaucracy.

name: Bleeding-Heart Libertarian
description: A libertarian with a conscience — free-market but deeply pluralist, universalist in morality, and optimistic about human nature. Anti-procedural, uncompromising, and anti-tribal. Empiricist-authentic in style — believes voluntary cooperation, not state action, best serves both individual liberty and human flourishing.

name: Burkean Steward
description: A Burkean conservative who values incremental change, institutional continuity, and narrow moral community. Proceduralist and culturally traditional, with an institutionalist epistemology and statesman aesthetic. Assimilationist on culture and cautious about human nature — prefers known arrangements over speculative social experiments.

name: Constitutional Conservative
description: A constitution-reverent conservative who pairs market-friendly economics with cultural traditionalism and strong procedural commitment. Assimilationist and moderately partisan, with institutionalist epistemology and statesman style. Values the written rules of the republic above any populist wave or progressive experiment.

name: Blackstone Conservative
description: A common-law conservative who combines free-market economics with cultural traditionalism and universalist morality. Deeply procedural, with an institutionalist epistemology and statesman aesthetic. Believes inherited legal institutions and precedent, more than any political ideology, safeguard liberty and social order.

name: Civic Traditionalist
description: A civic-minded traditionalist who values cultural continuity, community assimilation, and procedural respectability. Market-leaning and moderately engaged, with institutionalist epistemology and statesman style. Not a culture warrior or populist — just someone who believes stable traditions create the foundation for a decent civic life.

name: Responsible Conservative
description: A responsible conservative who prizes consensus, proceduralism, and institutional trust above ideological purity. Culturally traditional and assimilationist, with strong institutionalist epistemology and statesman aesthetic. Rejects tribalism and partisanship — sees conservatism as a temperament of responsibility, not a tribal identity.

name: Fiscal Gradualist
description: A fiscal conservative who advances market-friendly economics gradually, favoring compromise and patience over confrontation. Culturally progressive-leaning but low-intensity, competitive in worldview, and anti-tribal. Institutionalist and statesman in style — believes fiscal discipline is best achieved through steady, bipartisan reform.

name: Ordered Libertarian
description: An ordered libertarian who combines free-market economics with cultural traditionalism and a fierce rejection of compromise. Assimilationist but not strongly tribal, procedurally moderate, and institutionalist in epistemology. Values individual economic liberty within a framework of cultural order and established conventions.

name: Meritocratic Conservative
description: A meritocratic conservative who trusts markets to reward talent and hard work. Free-market, mildly traditional, optimistic about human potential, and anti-tribal. Institutionalist in epistemology and statesman in style — believes the system works for those who earn their place, and opposes leveling for its own sake.

name: National Developmentalist
description: A national developmentalist who favors state-guided market economics for national strength. Culturally traditional, assimilationist, and mildly competitive. Sees the system as broken and in need of structural reform, but through orderly, procedural means. Institutionalist and statesman — patriotic pragmatism, not populist grievance.

name: Heritage Guardian
description: A heritage defender who guards cultural tradition and community boundaries with assimilationist conviction. Traditionalist-pastoral in epistemology, system-critical, and tribal. Sees the system as broken not because it's unjust, but because it's abandoned the cultural inheritance that once held society together.

name: Altar-and-Hearth Conservative
description: An altar-and-hearth conservative centered on faith, family, and cultural continuity. Assimilationist, traditionalist-pastoral, and quietly outcome-focused. Not highly engaged or tribal — drawn to politics only insofar as it threatens the sacred institutions of religious community, moral order, and domestic life.

name: Closed Traditionalist
description: A sacred-order defender who views politics through the lens of transcendent moral and cultural authority. Assimilationist, culturally traditional, and zero-sum in worldview. Traditionalist-pastoral in epistemology and aesthetics — believes civilization rests on sacred foundations that progressive secularism threatens to destroy.

name: Civilizational Conservative
description: A civilizational conservative who sees Western culture as a besieged inheritance requiring active defense. Assimilationist, culturally traditional, system-critical, and deeply tribal. Highly engaged with a fighter aesthetic — less interested in policy details than in the grand struggle to preserve civilizational identity.

name: Customary Localist
description: A customary localist who grounds traditional values in place, habit, and local authority rather than national ideology. Assimilationist, free-market-leaning, culturally traditional, and deliberately disengaged from national politics. Traditionalist-pastoral in sensibility — prefers the known and the near to the abstract and the distant.

name: Duty Traditionalist
description: A duty-bound traditionalist who follows procedural rules and cultural custom with quiet conviction. Culturally traditional, assimilationist, and modestly engaged. Traditionalist-pastoral in both epistemology and style — sees political participation as a civic obligation rooted in inherited standards, not personal passion or tribal belonging.

name: Continuity Conservative
description: A continuity conservative who values cultural and procedural stability above all. Culturally traditional and assimilationist, with strong proceduralism and system-critique. Traditionalist-pastoral in sensibility — less interested in advancing any agenda than in preserving the institutional and cultural arrangements that already exist.

name: Gentle Traditionalist
description: A gentle traditionalist who combines cultural conservatism with genuine openness to compromise and pastoral warmth. Assimilationist but not combative, traditionalist-pastoral in epistemology and aesthetics, system-critical yet temperamentally moderate. Values continuity and community feeling over ideological confrontation or political engagement.

name: Integral Traditionalist
description: An integral traditionalist who insists on particularist moral boundaries and cultural conservatism. Zero-sum in worldview, deeply partisan, and strongly engaged. Traditionalist in epistemology and statesman in style — sees tradition not as nostalgia but as a living, total worldview that demands political loyalty and active defense.

name: Hobbesian Guardian
description: A Hobbesian realist who sees human nature as fundamentally flawed and politics as an arena of competing self-interests. Deeply zero-sum, pessimistic, and particularist in morality. Culturally traditional, assimilationist, and system-critical. Favors strong authority not from ideology, but from cold assessment of human nature.

name: Security Paternalist
description: A security-minded paternalist who prioritizes order and cultural assimilation. Competitive in worldview, system-critical, and institutionalist in epistemology. Not especially engaged or ideological — just wants a strong state that maintains cultural boundaries, enforces rules, and keeps society stable against internal and external threats.

name: Partisan Tribalist
description: A disciplined majoritarian who combines cultural assimilation with strong partisan and tribal identity. System-critical, procedural, and confident the majority should rule through established channels. Institutionalist in epistemology and statesman in style — believes the nation's majority culture deserves clear political representation.

name: Hard-State Manager
description: A hard-state manager who combines assimilationism and moral particularism with deep proceduralism and zero tolerance for compromise. System-critical and competitive, with a technocratic style. Believes strong, efficient state institutions — not democratic deliberation or cultural persuasion — are the answer to social disorder.

name: Emergency Orderist
description: An emergency-minded orderist who rejects compromise and sees the system as fundamentally broken. Assimilationist, particularist in morality, and deeply zero-sum. Highly engaged and institutionalist in epistemology — supports drastic measures to restore order, viewing the current crisis as too urgent for normal political processes.

name: Authority Pragmatist
description: A pragmatic authoritarian who values cultural assimilation, moral particularism, and system-critique without strong ideological passion. Competitive in worldview, moderately procedural, and consensus-tolerant. Institutionalist and statesman in style — supports strong authority not from zealotry but from practical assessment of what maintains order.

name: Anti-Elite Populist
description: An anti-elite populist who channels distrust of institutions through outcome-focused, uncompromising action. Assimilationist, particularist, and system-critical, with zero-sum worldview and intuitionist-authentic style. Rejects both proceduralism and consensus — the system is rigged, and only direct confrontation with elites can fix it.

name: Scarcity Populist
description: A scarcity-driven populist who combines redistributionist economics with cultural traditionalism, assimilationism, and deep pessimism. Anti-procedural, uncompromising, and zero-sum in worldview. Sees elites hoarding resources while ordinary people struggle. Fights with intuitionist conviction and a combative style born from material desperation.

name: Tribal Insurgent
description: A tribal insurgent who combines redistributionist economics with traditional culture, radical assimilationism, and total system rejection. Anti-procedural, uncompromising, and deeply zero-sum. Highly partisan, tribal, and engaged — views politics as existential group warfare where compromise equals surrender and institutions are enemy territory.

name: Embattled Majoritarian
description: An embattled majoritarian who feels cultural displacement and channels it through partisan loyalty and system-critique. Culturally traditional, assimilationist, and uncompromising, with a zero-sum worldview. Intuitionist-authentic in style — viscerally certain the majority's way of life is under siege from elites and outsiders.

name: Grievance Mobilizer
description: A grievance mobilizer who activates resentment against both elites and the system itself. Assimilationist, anti-procedural, uncompromising, and deeply pessimistic about human nature. Zero-sum and system-critical, with intuitionist epistemology and authentic style. Tribal and engaged — converts anger into organized political action.

name: National Protector
description: A national protector who combines cultural traditionalism and assimilationism with partisan loyalty and uncompromising stance. Zero-sum, system-critical, and intuitionist in epistemology. Fights with a combative aesthetic — sees the nation as threatened and believes only strong, decisive partisan leadership can defend it.

name: Combative Populist
description: A combative populist defined by total rejection of compromise, procedure, and moral universalism. Assimilationist, zero-sum, pessimistic about human nature, and system-critical. Deeply tribal and highly engaged, with intuitionist epistemology and fighter aesthetics. Politics is war — there are only allies and enemies.

name: Militant Partisan
description: A leader-centered insurgent who channels system rejection through devotion to a strong political figure. Assimilationist, particularist, zero-sum, and system-critical. Strongly partisan and highly engaged, with intuitionist-fighter style. Sees institutional channels as corrupt — only a decisive leader can break through and restore order.

name: Resentful Localist
description: A resentful localist who combines cultural traditionalism with deep anti-proceduralism, anti-compromise, and anti-partisan independence. Zero-sum, system-critical, and pessimistic about human nature. Intuitionist-authentic in style — neither left nor right, but angry at everyone above the local community level.

name: Alienated Outsider
description: An alienated outsider driven by moral conviction but total rejection of institutions and optimism. Anti-procedural, uncompromising, pessimistic, and disengaged. Autonomous in epistemology and authentic in style — trusts only personal experience and sees the political system as fundamentally hostile to genuine individual values.

name: Principled Abstainer
description: A principled abstainer who refuses political participation on moral grounds. Deeply universalist in morality but radically anti-procedural and anti-partisan. Optimistic about human nature yet convinced the political system corrupts everything it touches. Autonomous in epistemology and authentic in style — integrity requires withdrawal.

name: Cosmopolitan Nonconformist
description: A philosophical independent modeled on the ancient cynic — culturally progressive, pluralist, and anti-procedural, but fundamentally indifferent to most political questions. Anti-tribal, autonomous in epistemology, and authentic in style. Sees through political pretensions without bothering to offer alternatives.

name: Engaged Cosmopolitan
description: A contrarian intellectual who combines cultural pluralism and moral universalism with reflexive suspicion of procedure and consensus. Autonomous in epistemology and authentic in style — deeply engaged but allergic to tribal loyalty, ideological conformity, and institutional deference. Questions everything, joins nothing.

name: Quietist
description: A spiritual quietist who withdraws from political engagement on principle. Particularist in morality, anti-procedural, and deliberately disengaged and anti-tribal. Traditionalist-pastoral in epistemology and style — believes the political realm is spiritually corrosive and that the good life is found in contemplative withdrawal.

name: Quiet Middle
description: A quiet centrist who occupies the political middle with low engagement and minimal tribal attachment. Positive-sum and system-trusting, moderate across economics and culture. Institutionalist in epistemology and authentic in style — doesn't seek political identity, views the system as basically sound, and prefers to be left alone.

name: Comfortable Bystander
description: A comfortable bystander whose defining trait is extreme political disengagement. Procedurally inclined and system-trusting, but simply not interested enough to participate. Anti-tribal, particularist, and positive-sum by default — life is fine, the system works well enough, and politics is other people's business.

name: Survival Pragmatist
description: A survival pragmatist navigating politics through a lens of material vulnerability and cautious pessimism. Zero-sum, system-critical, and anti-tribal, with moderate engagement. Institutionalist in epistemology and authentic in style — not ideological, just trying to get by in a system they believe is stacked against ordinary people.

name: Apolitical Striver
description: An apolitical striver focused entirely on personal economic advancement. Free-market by instinct, culturally indifferent, and deliberately disengaged from political life. Anti-tribal and low on partisan identity — sees politics as irrelevant distraction from the real work of building a better life through individual effort.

name: Good Neighbor
description: A good neighbor whose politics flow from community bonds and moral universalism. Deeply pluralist yet accepting of cultural tradition, fiercely anti-tribal and system-trusting. Consensus-seeking with a statesman aesthetic — engages politics not from ideology but from a genuine desire to help others and preserve social harmony.

name: Spectator Citizen
description: A spectator citizen who watches politics with genuine interest but limited participation. Moderate and universalist, deeply procedural and empiricist, but not highly engaged. Anti-tribal, authentic in style — follows political developments like a thoughtful observer, forming evidence-based opinions without feeling compelled to act on them.

name: Civic Minimalist
description: A civic minimalist who fulfills basic political duties without deeper engagement. Procedurally inclined, anti-tribal, and culturally indifferent. Institutionalist in epistemology and authentic in style — believes in following the rules but sees no reason to invest emotional energy or identity in political participation beyond that.

name: Latent Alarmist
description: A crisis-activated sleeper who is normally disengaged but awakens when the system feels threatened. System-critical with anti-tribal instincts, moderate across most dimensions. Institutionalist and authentic in style — usually content to stay on the sidelines until perceived crisis triggers reluctant but genuine political engagement.

name: Reluctant Partisan
description: A reluctant partisan who votes with a party despite deep ambivalence about political engagement. Anti-tribal and low-energy, with procedural instincts and system-critique. Institutionalist and authentic in style — maintains partisan affiliation out of habit or perceived necessity, not enthusiasm or tribal belonging.

name: Uncompromising Activist
description: A single-issue activator who channels intense political energy into one cause while remaining indifferent to broader politics. Highly engaged but uncompromising and non-tribal. Institutionalist and authentic in style — their one issue burns hot enough to demand action, while everything else barely registers.

name: Tribal Loyalist
description: A tribal loyalist whose politics is defined entirely by group allegiance rather than ideology. Strongly partisan, deeply tribal, and uncompromising in defending their side. Institutionalist and authentic in style — doesn't engage with policy details, just knows which team they're on and refuses to budge.

name: Loyal Democrat
description: A loyal Democrat whose political identity is party membership rather than specific ideology. Mildly redistributionist and pluralist, with moderate tribal and partisan attachment. Institutionalist and authentic in style — votes Democratic consistently not from deep conviction on issues but from inherited identification with the party.

name: Loyal Republican
description: A loyal Republican whose political identity flows from party attachment rather than detailed policy positions. Mildly market-friendly and culturally traditional, with strong partisan identity. Institutionalist and authentic in style — supports the GOP as a matter of identity and loyalty, not ideological precision.

name: Duty Voter
description: A duty voter who participates in politics from civic obligation rather than passion or tribal loyalty. Culturally traditional, morally universalist, and procedurally committed. Anti-tribal and system-critical, with institutionalist epistemology and authentic style. Votes because citizens should, not because they expect it to change much.

name: Negative Partisan
description: A negative partisan motivated not by love of their own side but by fear and loathing of the opposition. Zero-sum, system-critical, and strongly partisan without clear ideological positions. Institutionalist and authentic in style — their political identity is defined entirely by what they're against, not what they're for.

name: Progressive Civic Nationalist
description: An economically progressive patriot who believes the nation should live up to its ideals. Redistributionist, pluralist, procedural, and consensus-seeking, with strong tribal bonds. Highly engaged with intuitionist epistemology and visionary style. Works within the system but fights hard for economic justice and national purpose.

name: Disruptive Cosmopolitan
description: A disruptive cosmopolitan who pairs free-market economics with cultural progressivism and anti-procedural instincts. Particularist in morality, pluralist, and uncompromising. Optimistic about human nature, non-partisan, and non-tribal. Empiricist and statesman in style — wants to shake up sclerotic institutions while building something genuinely global.

name: Aspirational Traditionalist
description: An aspirational traditionalist who combines free-market economics with deep moral universalism and traditionalist-pastoral sensibility. Culturally progressive-leaning but rooted in tradition, moderate in proceduralism and engagement. Not tribal or partisan — sees moral ambition and economic striving as complementary paths to the good life.

name: Moral Crusader
description: A prophetic revivalist driven by visionary moral conviction and maximum political engagement. Deeply universalist, culturally traditional, zero-sum in worldview, and strongly partisan-tribal. Nihilist in epistemology and visionary in style — sees politics as a spiritual calling demanding total commitment to moral and cultural renewal.

name: Optimistic Challenger
description: A holistic dissenter who combines anti-procedural conviction with optimism about human nature and a nihilist-revelatory epistemology. Zero-sum in worldview, engaged, and authentic in style. Rejects institutional authority while believing deeply in human potential — dissents not from cynicism but from holistic spiritual conviction.

name: Civic Assimilationist
description: A civic assimilationist who pairs market-friendly economics with cultural traditionalism and pluralist values. Procedural, institutionalist-technocratic, and moderately engaged. Believes in cultural integration through shared civic institutions rather than coerced conformity — assimilation as aspiration, not exclusion.

name: Market Green Modernist
description: A market green modernist who combines market-friendly economics with cultural progressivism, deep system-critique, and empiricist conviction. Optimistic about human nature, procedural, and consensus-inclined. Non-partisan and non-tribal, with a technocratic style — believes market mechanisms and scientific evidence can solve the environmental crisis.

name: Black Voter
description: An identity-anchored voter whose politics flow primarily from Black communal experience. Redistributionist on economics but defined less by ideology than by racial-group solidarity and collective memory. High tribal fusion means party loyalty tracks community consensus. Authentic in style, skeptical of systems that promised neutrality but delivered exclusion.

name: White Grievance Voter
description: An identity-anchored voter whose politics are organized around perceived white cultural displacement. High cultural uniformity and zero-sum orientation signal a threat-driven worldview. Tribal fusion is intense and partisan — politics is experienced as defense of an embattled in-group. Fighter aesthetic, low system-trust, and deep suspicion of cosmopolitan elites.

name: Evangelical Voter
description: An identity-anchored voter whose political identity is inseparable from evangelical Christian faith. Traditionalist epistemology and narrow moral circle reflect scripture-based certainty. Tribal fusion is church-community deep, and partisan attachment follows religious leadership cues. Pastoral aesthetic — politics as stewardship of a divinely ordered society.

name: LGBTQ Voter
description: An identity-anchored voter whose politics are organized around LGBTQ communal experience and rights. Progressive on culture and moral circle, with autonomous epistemology reflecting hard-won independence from institutional authority. High tribal fusion to queer community, strong partisan attachment. Authentic aesthetic — values visibility and lived experience over procedural abstraction.

name: Feminist Voter
description: An identity-anchored voter whose politics center on gender equity as a structural critique. Progressive on culture and moral circle, with high system-ontology reflecting belief that patriarchy is systemic. Strong tribal fusion to feminist community, deep partisan attachment. Procedural and engaged — sees institutional reform as the mechanism for dismantling gendered hierarchy.

name: Male Grievance Voter
description: An identity-anchored voter whose politics are organized around perceived male status-threat. High zero-sum orientation and cultural traditionalism signal a grievance-driven worldview. Strong tribal fusion to masculinist community, with intense partisan attachment. Fighter aesthetic, low system-trust — experiences gender progress as personal loss rather than collective gain.
